title: A Fake Artist Goes to New York

walkthrough.title: Choose the Question Master
walkthrough.text: Welcome to A Fake Artist Goes to New York. This is a drawing and social deduction party game where everyone draws together on a shared canvas — but one player has no idea what they are drawing. Let's get set up. First, choose one player to be the Question Master. The Question Master runs the round but does not draw. They are the host, the secret-keeper, and the judge all rolled into one.

walkthrough.title: Pick a Category and Word
walkthrough.text: The Question Master secretly picks a category and a word that fits it. For example, the category might be "animal" and the word might be "elephant." The category will be announced to everyone, but the word must stay hidden until the round plays out. The Question Master should pick a word that is not too easy and not too hard — something most people could draw but that requires a few strokes to convey.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Cards
walkthrough.text: The Question Master takes one small card for each player. On all but one card, the Question Master writes the secret word. On the remaining card, the Question Master writes a large "X" instead. This card belongs to the Fake Artist. The Question Master shuffles the cards face-down so nobody knows which card has the X.

walkthrough.title: Deal Cards and Grab Markers
walkthrough.text: The Question Master deals one card face-down to each player. Each player peeks at their card secretly. If you see the word, you are a Real Artist. If you see an X, you are the Fake Artist — and you must hide this fact. Each player also takes a unique colored marker so everyone can tell whose strokes are whose. Place a large sheet of paper or a canvas in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Announce the Category
walkthrough.text: The Question Master announces the category out loud to everyone. For example, "The category is animal." Everyone now knows the topic area, but only the Real Artists know the exact word. The Fake Artist only knows the category. Let's begin drawing.

summary.title: Choose the Question Master
bullets: 1 player becomes the Question Master (QM)
QM runs the round but does not draw

summary.title: Pick a Category and Word
bullets: QM secretly picks a category (e.g., "animal") and a word (e.g., "elephant")
Category is public, word is secret
Choose medium-difficulty words

summary.title: Prepare the Cards
bullets: 1 card per player — all show the secret word except 1
1 card gets an "X" instead → this is the Fake Artist's card
QM shuffles cards face-down

summary.title: Deal Cards and Grab Markers
bullets: Deal 1 card face-down to each player → peek secretly
Word = Real Artist, X = Fake Artist
Each player takes a unique colored marker
Place a shared canvas/paper in the center

summary.title: Announce the Category
bullets: QM announces the category out loud (e.g., "animal")
Real Artists know the word; Fake Artist only knows the category
Drawing begins

walkthrough.title: Two Drawing Rounds
walkthrough.text: In each round, every player takes one turn to draw on the shared canvas. The Question Master announces the drawing order, going clockwise around the table. After everyone has drawn once, the second round begins and everyone draws again in the same order.

walkthrough.title: One Continuous Stroke Per Turn
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you draw exactly one continuous stroke on the shared canvas. That means your marker touches the paper, you draw, and then you lift it. Once your marker leaves the paper, your turn is over. You cannot go back and add more. Your stroke can be a line, a curve, a shape, or anything else — but it must be one unbroken movement.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Restrictions
walkthrough.text: There are two things you are never allowed to draw. You cannot write letters or numbers, and you cannot use symbols like arrows or check marks. Everything must be freehand illustration. These rules apply to everyone — Real Artists and the Fake Artist alike.

walkthrough.title: Discussion and Voting
walkthrough.text: After both drawing rounds are complete, everyone discusses the artwork. Look at each player's strokes by their marker color. Who drew something suspicious? Who seemed confident? Who seemed lost? After a brief discussion, everyone votes simultaneously by pointing at the player they think is the Fake Artist. The Question Master does not vote.

walkthrough.title: Majority Rules
walkthrough.text: Count the votes. The player with the most votes is accused of being the Fake Artist. If there is a tie, the tied players each give a short defense, and then everyone votes again between just the tied players. Only a clear majority leads to an accusation.

walkthrough.title: Fake Artist Caught — The Final Guess
walkthrough.text: If the accused player is indeed the Fake Artist, the round is not over yet. The Fake Artist gets one last chance to win. They must guess the exact secret word. If the Fake Artist guesses correctly, the Fake Artist and the Question Master both win. If the Fake Artist guesses wrong, the Real Artists win.

walkthrough.title: Fake Artist Not Caught
walkthrough.text: If the group accuses the wrong player — meaning a Real Artist gets the most votes — the Fake Artist and the Question Master both win immediately. The Real Artists have been fooled. The Fake Artist reveals their X card and everyone can see exactly which strokes belonged to the imposter.

walkthrough.title: Next Round
walkthrough.text: After the round ends, a new player becomes the Question Master, and the game starts fresh with a new category, a new word, and new cards. Play as many rounds as you like, giving everyone a chance to be the Question Master. The fun is in the reveals and the bluffing, not in keeping score.

walkthrough.title: Welcome — A Practice Round
walkthrough.text: We will see how the Real Artists try to prove they know the word without giving it away, how the Fake Artist tries to blend in, and the three classic mistakes that trip up new players. Our seven players are Alex, Bea, Carlos, Dana, Ellie, Frank, and Grace. Alex is the Question Master this round.

walkthrough.title: Setup — Cards Are Dealt
walkthrough.text: Alex picks the category "food" and the secret word "pizza." Alex writes "pizza" on five cards and "X" on one card, shuffles them, and deals one to each of the six drawing players. Everyone peeks at their card. Frank sees an X — he is the Fake Artist. Everyone else sees "pizza." Alex announces, "The category is food." Frank knows the topic is food, but has no idea which food. Let's draw.

walkthrough.title: Round One — Bea Starts
walkthrough.text: Bea goes first. She knows the word is pizza, so she draws a large circle. It is clearly the outline of a pizza, but it could also be a plate, an orange, a cookie, or dozens of other round foods. This is smart. She proved she knows the word without spelling it out for the Fake Artist. The other Real Artists nod — they see where this is going.

walkthrough.title: Round One — Carlos and Dana Add Detail
walkthrough.text: Carlos draws a triangle shape inside Bea's circle — a pizza slice. Still ambiguous enough that Frank might not catch on immediately. Dana adds a small cluster of circles on top of the triangle — pepperoni. The drawing is starting to look unmistakably like pizza, but each individual stroke was subtle. This is the art of being a Real Artist: contributing without revealing.

walkthrough.title: Round One — Ellie Goes Too Far
walkthrough.text: Ellie is excited. She draws a detailed wavy crust edge along part of Bea's circle, complete with little bumps and texture marks. It is beautiful — and way too specific. Everyone immediately sees "pizza crust," and more importantly, so does Frank. Ellie just handed the Fake Artist a huge clue.

walkthrough.title: Lesson — Do Not Over-Reveal
walkthrough.text: Real Artists must draw recognizably enough that other Real Artists can verify they know the word. But drawing too specifically hands the answer to the Fake Artist on a silver platter. If Frank now draws something pizza-related, nobody will suspect him — because Ellie made it obvious. Keep your strokes meaningful but ambiguous. Let the Real Artists read between the lines while keeping the Fake Artist guessing.

walkthrough.title: Round One — Frank the Fake Artist Draws
walkthrough.text: Thanks to Ellie's crust, Frank now knows the word is almost certainly pizza. He confidently draws a small triangle with lines radiating from the top — clearly meant to be a pizza slice with steam rising. It looks natural and fits perfectly with the drawing. Nobody bats an eye. Frank has survived Round One completely undetected because another player's oversharing saved him.

walkthrough.title: Round One — Grace Finishes
walkthrough.text: Grace draws a small square shape near the bottom of the canvas — a pizza box. Subtle and clever. Round one is complete. Every player has one stroke on the canvas. Time for Round Two.

walkthrough.title: Round Two — Building On the Canvas
walkthrough.text: In Round Two, players add a second stroke each. Bea adds dividing lines inside her circle to show slices. Carlos adds a small leaf shape — basil. Dana draws a wavy drizzle line — sauce or cheese. Each stroke adds detail without screaming the answer. They are being more careful now, having seen how Ellie's over-detailed crust nearly ruined the round.

walkthrough.title: Round Two — Frank Copies Too Closely
walkthrough.text: Frank is feeling confident, so he draws another pepperoni circle — mimicking exactly what Dana drew in Round One. It is a safe move, but it is also suspicious. Frank did not contribute anything original. He just copied an existing element. Grace narrows her eyes. She noticed.

walkthrough.title: Lesson — Copying Is Suspicious
walkthrough.text: When the Fake Artist figures out the word mid-round, their instinct is to copy what others have already drawn. But Real Artists who know the word from the start tend to add new details, not duplicate existing ones. Copying is one of the biggest tells in this game. If you are the Fake Artist and you have figured out the word, add a detail that nobody else has drawn yet. Show originality, not imitation.

walkthrough.title: Discussion — The Accusations Fly
walkthrough.text: Drawing is over. Alex opens the discussion. Grace immediately says, "Frank just drew another pepperoni — he did not add anything new." Bea agrees. But Ellie defends Frank, saying his first stroke was a perfect pizza slice. Carlos points out that Ellie's crust was suspiciously detailed — maybe she was overcompensating? The group debates. Suspicion lands on both Frank and Ellie.

walkthrough.title: The Vote — Frank Is Caught
walkthrough.text: Alex counts to three and everyone points. Four fingers point at Frank. Two point at Ellie. Frank is accused. He flips his card and reveals the X — he is the Fake Artist. But the round is not over yet. Frank gets to guess the exact word. He says "pizza" immediately — thanks to Ellie's crust, he knew it for sure. Frank guesses correctly, so Frank and Alex win the round. The Real Artists groan.

walkthrough.title: Lesson — Protect the Word, Not Just Yourself
walkthrough.text: The Real Artists caught the Fake Artist but still lost because Frank could guess the word. This is the deepest lesson in the game. It is not enough to identify the Fake Artist — you also need to keep the word secret enough that even a caught Fake Artist cannot guess it. If Ellie had drawn something more subtle instead of that detailed crust, Frank might have been caught and unable to guess "pizza." Every Real Artist shares responsibility for protecting the word.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw three key mistakes: Ellie over-revealing the word with her detailed crust, Frank copying an existing detail instead of adding something original, and the group failing to protect the word even after catching the Fake Artist. Remember these lessons and you will be a sharper player from your very first real round.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first real round, here are two things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for advice on drawing strategy, bluffing as the Fake Artist, and picking good words as the Question Master.

Second — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun fooling your friends.

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → drawing strategy, bluffing, word selection
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Real Artists — Be Subtle But Clear
walkthrough.text: As a Real Artist, your goal is to prove to the other Real Artists that you know the word — without giving the answer away to the Fake Artist. Draw things that are recognizable if you already know the word, but ambiguous if you do not. For example, if the word is "guitar," draw a curved line that could be the body — not the full outline with strings and tuning pegs. Let your fellow Real Artists connect the dots.

walkthrough.title: Fake Artist — Observe Before You Draw
walkthrough.text: As the Fake Artist, your most powerful tool is observation. Watch what others draw before your turn. Look at the shapes, the positions on the canvas, and how players react to each other's strokes. By the time your turn comes, you may have enough context to draw something that fits. Draw with confidence — hesitation draws suspicion. Even if you are not sure what the word is, commit to your stroke.

walkthrough.title: Fake Artist — Add to the Drawing, Don't Copy It
walkthrough.text: One of the biggest tells for a Fake Artist is copying what someone else has already drawn. Real Artists who know the word naturally add new elements. If you are the Fake Artist and you have figured out the word, prove it by adding a detail nobody else has contributed. If you have not figured out the word, draw something abstract that could connect to many possible answers within the category.

walkthrough.title: Question Master — Choose the Right Difficulty
walkthrough.text: The Question Master sets the tone for the entire round. Pick words that are specific enough to draw but broad enough that the Fake Artist cannot guess from the category alone. Avoid words that are too easy — like "sun" for the category "space" — because the Fake Artist will guess it instantly if caught. Avoid words that are too hard to draw, because Real Artists will struggle and look suspicious themselves.

walkthrough.title: Watch the First Stroke Carefully
walkthrough.text: The first player to draw sets the foundation for the entire canvas. Pay close attention to their stroke. A Real Artist's first stroke is usually a confident structural element — the outline of the object, a key feature. A Fake Artist's first stroke tends to be vague or overly generic. If you are the first to draw as a Real Artist, start with something foundational but not too revealing.

walkthrough.title: Use Discussion Time Wisely
walkthrough.text: The discussion phase is just as important as the drawing phase. Ask players to explain what they drew and why. Real Artists can usually describe their intent clearly. Fake Artists often give vague or evasive answers. But be careful — some Real Artists are just bad at explaining, and some Fake Artists are great liars. Listen to tone and specificity, not just the words.

walkthrough.title: Remember — Catching Is Not Enough
walkthrough.text: Even if the group identifies the Fake Artist, the Fake Artist can still win by guessing the exact word. This means Real Artists must protect the word throughout the game. Do not draw so obviously that a caught Fake Artist can easily name the word. The best outcome for Real Artists is catching the Fake Artist AND having kept the word ambiguous enough that the guess fails.

walkthrough.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
walkthrough.text: Watch out for these three common mistakes. First, Real Artists drawing too specifically and handing the word to the Fake Artist. Second, the Fake Artist panicking and drawing something completely unrelated to the canvas. Third, players focusing only on catching the Fake Artist while forgetting to protect the word. Avoid these and you will have a much better game.

walkthrough.title: The Ambiguity Spectrum
walkthrough.text: Every stroke you make as a Real Artist sits on a spectrum from too vague to too specific. Too vague and your teammates might think you are the Fake Artist. Too specific and the Fake Artist learns the word. The sweet spot is a stroke that makes perfect sense to someone who knows the word but could mean several different things to someone who does not. Master this balance and you will win far more rounds as a Real Artist.

walkthrough.title: Strategic First Strokes
walkthrough.text: If you draw first, you have enormous power. A well-chosen first stroke frames the entire canvas. Draw something that anchors the word visually — like a horizon line for "mountain" or a circle for "basketball" — but could plausibly fit multiple words in the category. If you draw last in Round One, you have a different advantage: you have seen everyone else's strokes and can assess who seems lost.

walkthrough.title: Bait and Misdirect as the Fake Artist
walkthrough.text: Advanced Fake Artists do not just try to blend in — they actively misdirect. If you have narrowed down the word to two or three possibilities, draw a stroke that fits your best guess while also casting subtle suspicion on a Real Artist. During discussion, confidently question another player's stroke — "Why did you draw that? It does not look like it fits." Shifting suspicion is as valuable as blending in.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table, Not Just the Canvas
walkthrough.text: Advanced players watch reactions as much as they watch the drawing. Does a player look relieved when someone else draws? Do they hesitate before starting their stroke? Do they glance at the canvas nervously or study it calmly? Body language tells you more than the strokes themselves. The Fake Artist often watches other players' faces for confirmation, while Real Artists tend to focus on the canvas.

walkthrough.title: Question Master Word Selection Mastery
walkthrough.text: The Question Master controls the game's difficulty entirely through word selection. Great words have multiple drawable features so Real Artists can contribute unique strokes. Great words also have close neighbors in the category that could trick a Fake Artist into guessing wrong. For example, in the category "instrument," the word "violin" is excellent because a caught Fake Artist might guess "guitar" or "cello." A word like "drum" is too simple — too few strokes needed and too easy to guess.

walkthrough.title: The Verification Stroke
walkthrough.text: In Round Two, experienced Real Artists use their second stroke as a verification signal. Instead of adding more detail to the main drawing, they draw a secondary element that only makes sense if you know the word — like a small detail from the word's context rather than the object itself. This separates genuine knowledge from lucky guessing. If another player's second stroke seems randomly placed or disconnected, that is a strong Fake Artist signal.

walkthrough.title: Controlling Information Flow
walkthrough.text: The best Real Artist teams instinctively coordinate how much information to reveal each round. In Round One, everyone draws broad structural strokes. In Round Two, everyone adds distinctive but not definitive details. No single stroke gives the word away, but taken together, the drawing is clearly the right answer. If one player breaks this pattern by drawing too much detail too early, it benefits the Fake Artist. Police your own strokes and gently call out over-revealers during discussion.

walkthrough.title: The Meta-Game — Patterns Across Rounds
walkthrough.text: Over multiple rounds, players develop habits. Some players always draw timidly when they are the Fake Artist. Some always draw boldly to compensate. Some Question Masters pick words from the same difficulty range every time. Pay attention to these patterns. If you notice a player who is usually confident suddenly drawing cautiously, that shift in behavior is more telling than the stroke itself. The meta-game rewards long-term observation and memory.

summary.title: The Ambiguity Spectrum
bullets: Every stroke sits between too vague and too specific
Sweet spot: makes sense if you know, ambiguous if you don't
Master this balance to win more as a Real Artist

Fake Artists watch faces for confirmation; Real Artists focus on canvas

entries.definition: The player who runs the current round. The QM picks the category and secret word, writes the cards, deals them out, announces the category, and moderates the discussion. The QM does not draw and does not vote. If the Fake Artist wins, the QM also wins.

Any player who receives a card with the secret word written on it. Real Artists know what to draw and must prove their knowledge through their strokes while keeping the word ambiguous enough that the Fake Artist cannot guess it if caught.

The player who receives the card marked with an X instead of the secret word. The Fake Artist knows only the category and must bluff their way through two drawing rounds without being identified. If caught, the Fake Artist gets one chance to guess the word.

Each player draws exactly one continuous stroke per turn. The marker must touch the paper, draw without lifting, and then lift once to end the turn. The stroke can be any shape — line, curve, circle, squiggle — but it must be a single unbroken movement.

Players cannot write letters, numbers, or use recognizable symbols like arrows, check marks, or mathematical signs. All drawing must be freehand illustration. This rule applies to both Real Artists and the Fake Artist.

Each player uses a unique colored marker so that all strokes can be traced back to the player who drew them. This is essential during discussion and voting, as players examine which colored strokes look suspicious or out of place.
The game consists of exactly two drawing rounds. In each round, every player draws one stroke on the shared canvas in clockwise order. After both rounds, the canvas has two strokes from each player.

After discussion, all players (except the QM) vote at the same time by pointing at the player they suspect is the Fake Artist. The player with the most votes is accused. In a tie, tied players give a short defense and a revote is held.

The player who receives the most votes during the simultaneous vote is accused of being the Fake Artist. If they are indeed the Fake Artist, the game proceeds to the final guess. If an innocent player is accused, the Fake Artist and QM win immediately.

When the Fake Artist is correctly identified, they get one last chance to win by guessing the exact secret word. A correct guess means the Fake Artist and QM win. A wrong guess means the Real Artists win. This mechanic forces Real Artists to protect the word, not just catch the Fake Artist.

Drawing too specifically as a Real Artist, making the word obvious to the Fake Artist. This is one of the most common mistakes. Even if the Fake Artist is caught, they can use the over-revealed information to guess the word correctly and still win.

The Fake Artist's primary strategy of drawing strokes that appear natural and consistent with the other players' contributions. Successful blending involves observing other strokes, drawing confidently, and contributing something original rather than copying.

A Round 2 strategy where Real Artists draw a contextual or secondary detail related to the word rather than adding more to the main image. This helps distinguish genuine knowledge from lucky guessing, as only someone who truly knows the word would think to add contextual elements.

An advanced Fake Artist technique of actively shifting suspicion toward other players during discussion. This can involve questioning another player's stroke choices, pointing out their hesitation, or casting doubt on their contributions to the drawing.
